title: Antiphospholipid Syndrome: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Antiphospholipid Syndrome
Identify key diagnostic features of Antiphospholipid Syndrome
Apply clinical reasoning to Antiphospholipid Syndrome management
Recognize common pitfalls in Antiphospholipid Syndrome

sections.title: Overview of Antiphospholipid Syndrome
sections.content:
**Antiphospholipid Syndrome**

Thrombosis paradox (in vitro anticoagulant, in vivo procoagulant), pregnancy morbidity, lupus anticoagulant, diagnosis criteria

**Key Points:**
• Arterial AND venous thrombosis + pregnancy morbidity
• Antibodies: lupus anticoagulant (strongest predictor of thrombosis), anticardiolipin, anti-β2-glycoprotein I
• Paradox: 'anticoagulant' name because it prolongs PTT in vitro, but CAUSES thrombosis in vivo

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Arterial AND venous thrombosis + pregnancy morbidity
• Antibodies: lupus anticoagulant (strongest predictor of thrombosis), anticardiolipin, anti-β2-glycoprotein I

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Paradox: 'anticoagulant' name because it prolongs PTT in vitro, but CAUSES thrombosis in vivo
• Diagnosis: clinical criterion + laboratory criterion (positive on 2 occasions ≥12 weeks apart)
• Treatment: lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin (NOT DOACs for triple-positive APS); heparin + aspirin in pregnancy

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Antiphospholipid Syndrome:**

• Paradox: 'anticoagulant' name because it prolongs PTT in vitro, but CAUSES thrombosis in vivo
• Diagnosis: clinical criterion + laboratory criterion (positive on 2 occasions ≥12 weeks apart)
• Treatment: lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin (NOT DOACs for triple-positive APS); heparin + aspirin in pregnancy

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Antiphospholipid Syndrome?

options.text: Arterial AND venous thrombosis + pregnancy morbidity
options.explanation: Arterial AND venous thrombosis + pregnancy morbidity

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Antiphospholipid Syndrome?

options.text: Treatment: lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin (NOT DOACs for triple-positive APS); heparin + aspirin in pregnancy
options.explanation: Treatment: lifelong anticoagulation with warfarin (NOT DOACs for triple-positive APS); heparin + aspirin in pregnancy

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Antiphospholipid Syndrome. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Antibodies: lupus anticoagulant (strongest predictor of thrombosis), anticardiolipin, anti-β2-glycoprotein I
options.explanation: Antibodies: lupus anticoagulant (strongest predictor of thrombosis), anticardiolipin, anti-β2-glycoprotein I

mcqs.stem: A 65-year-old woman with new-onset headache, jaw claudication, and ESR of 85 mm/hr is suspected of having giant cell arteritis. What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Start high-dose prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) immediately, then arrange temporal artery biopsy
options.explanation: Per ACR/EULAR guidelines, GCA with visual symptoms is an emergency. High-dose glucocorticoids must be started IMMEDIATELY to prevent irreversible vision loss. Temporal artery biopsy remains diagnostic even after several weeks of steroid therapy due to persistent inflammatory changes.

options.text: Schedule temporal artery biopsy first to confirm diagnosis before starting treatment
options.explanation: Waiting for biopsy before treatment risks permanent vision loss. Biopsy can be performed within 2 weeks of starting steroids without significantly affecting sensitivity.

options.text: Start low-dose prednisone (10 mg/day) and monitor symptoms for 1 week
options.explanation: Low-dose steroids are insufficient for GCA. The recommended dose is 40-60 mg/day for uncomplicated GCA, or 1 mg/kg/day (up to IV pulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3 days) when visual symptoms are present.

options.text: Order CRP and IL-6 levels before making treatment decisions
options.explanation: While CRP and IL-6 are elevated in GCA, additional testing should not delay treatment in a patient with classic symptoms and elevated ESR. Vision loss can occur within hours.
